description: This figure presents the morphology and types of inclusions in 1# Fe-15Mn-1Al-C low-density steel, characterized via scanning electron microscopy and energy dispersive spectroscopy elemental mapping. Subfigures (a) to (e) show single-particle inclusions: elongated Al₂O₃ (>8 μm in length), near-elliptical AlN, elongated AlN (~12 μm), single-particle MnS, and angular TiN respectively. Subfigures (f) to (i) display composite inclusions: core-shell structured Al₂O₃-MnS, AlN-MnS composite with matching Al/N/Mn/S elemental distribution maps, TiN-MnS composite with clear interphase interface, and ternary Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS composite with matching Mn/S/Al/N/O/Fe elemental distribution maps. Most subfigures use a 4 μm scale bar, while subfigure (h) uses a 5 μm scale bar. This figure confirms the coexistence of multiple single and composite inclusions in the 1# sample, providing direct morphological and compositional evidence for inclusion formation and heterogeneous nucleation mechanisms in low-density steels.

This group of figures presents the morphology and phase identification of inclusions in 2# Fe-15Mn-3Al-C low-density steel, combining scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping. Subfigures (a)-(c) show single-phase inclusions: (a) ~23 μm elongated AlN, (b) octahedral MnS, (c) boot-shaped TiN, with clearly marked scale bars of 10 μm, 4 μm, 3 μm respectively. Subfigures (d)-(g) display composite multi-phase inclusions: (d) Al₂O₃-MnS, (e) AlN-MnS supported by corresponding Al, N, Mn, S elemental maps verifying the tight binding between two phases, (f) core-shell structured Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS with Al₂O₃ core, AlN interlayer and MnS outer layer, (g) TiN-MnS validated by S, Mn, Ti, N elemental maps. These results demonstrate the coexistence of single and composite inclusions in the steel, providing direct morphological and compositional evidence for the heterogeneous nucleation formation mechanism of inclusions.

This figure presents the morphology and corresponding energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping of inclusions in the 3# Fe-15Mn-5Al-C low-density steel. Subfigures (a, b) show polyhedral single or aggregated AlN inclusions with a 10 μm scale bar. Subfigures (c, d) display octahedral MnS inclusions with size around 10 μm, with a 10 μm scale bar. Subfigures (e, f) exhibit elongated TiN inclusions with length over 30 μm, with a 10 μm scale bar. Subfigures (g-i) demonstrate composite inclusions, including Al₂O₃-MnS (Al₂O₃ at the bottom, MnS on the top, 2 μm scale bar), TiN-MnS (MnS attached on TiN, 2 μm scale bar), and AlN-MnS composite inclusions (10 μm scale bar). EDS mappings of Al, N, Mn, S, Ti, O confirm the phase compositions of each inclusion, verifying the coexistence of single-phase and composite inclusions in the steel, and supporting the heterogeneous nucleation mechanism between different inclusion phases.

This is a stacked bar chart displaying the number percentage of different types of inclusions in three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples, labeled 1# (0.868 wt% Al), 2# (2.766 wt% Al), and 3# (4.695 wt% Al) with increasing Al content. The y-axis represents the number percentage of inclusions (%), and the counted inclusion types include single-phase inclusions (Al₂O₃, AlN, MnS, TiN) and composite inclusions (Al₂O₃-MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS). Clear trends are observed: as Al content increases, the number percentage of single Al₂O₃ decreases, single AlN increases, Al₂O₃-MnS composite inclusions increase, while AlN-MnS and TiN-MnS composite inclusions decrease, and TiN content shows no obvious change. The ternary Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS composite inclusions only exist in 1# and 2# samples, and are absent in 3# sample with the highest Al content. This figure supports the conclusion that Al content significantly regulates the type and quantity distribution of inclusions in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, and high Al addition inhibits the formation of ternary oxide-nitride-sulfide composite inclusions.

This bar chart illustrates the size distribution of inclusions in three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples, i.e., 1# (≈0.868 wt% Al), 2# (≈2.766 wt% Al), 3# (≈4.695 wt% Al). The x-axis denotes inclusion size intervals of 1-5 μm, 5-10 μm, and >10 μm, while the y-axis represents the number percentage of inclusions. Specific measured data are: 58.4%, 32.3%, 9.4% for 1# sample in the three size intervals respectively; 53.6%, 34.6%, 11.8% for 2# sample; 48.2%, 46.9%, 4.9% for 3# sample. A clear trend can be concluded: with increasing Al content, the number percentage of inclusions in the 1-5 μm range decreases, that in the 5-10 μm range increases, and that in the >10 μm range first rises then drops. This figure supports the core conclusion that rising Al content modifies the solidification route of the experimental steel, thus affecting the nucleation, growth and size distribution of inclusions.

This group of three subplots presents FactSage 8.2 calculated phase diagrams of the C-Si-Mn-P-S-N-O-Al-Ti-Fe system for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples: 1# with 1 wt% Al, 2# with 3 wt% Al, and 3# with 5 wt% Al. The horizontal axis represents C content in wt%, and the vertical axis represents temperature in K. Each subplot marks the liquidus temperature (red dot) and solidus temperature (gray dot) at the fixed C content of ~0.4 wt% for the corresponding sample. With the increase of Al content, the liquidus temperature rises (1# ~1702 K, 2# ~1740 K, 3# ~1796 K) while the solidus temperature decreases (1# ~1588 K, 2# ~1560 K, 3# ~1532 K), the austenite (FCC) phase region narrows, and the ferrite (BCC) phase region expands. The solidification route changes from L→L+FCC→FCC for 1# to routes passing through the BCC phase region for 2# and 3#. This figure supports the conclusion that Al content modifies the solidification behavior of the steel, which further influences the precipitation behavior of inclusions during solidification.

This figure (Figure 7) plots the relationship between logK<sub>AlN</sub> (solid curves, logarithm of AlN equilibrium constant), logQ<sub>AlN</sub> (dashed curves, logarithm of AlN activity product), and solidification fraction z<sub>s</sub> for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples: 1# with ~1 wt% Al, 2# with ~3 wt% Al, 3# with ~5 wt% Al. The intersection points of corresponding logK and logQ curves give the solidification fraction of AlN precipitation, which are 0.46 for 1#, 0.22 for 2#, and 0.51 for 3#, respectively. It shows that AlN precipitation advances when Al content increases from 1 wt% to 3 wt%, while it delays when Al content further increases to 5 wt%. This figure supports the conclusion that Al content variation alters the solidification route of the steel, thus changing the precipitation behavior of AlN and other inclusions during solidification.

The figure plots the segregation ratios (C_L/C_0) of Al and N elements against solidification fraction Z_s for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples (1# with 0.868 wt% Al, 2# with 2.766 wt% Al, 3# with 4.695 wt% Al) during solidification, with an inset magnifying the region at Z_s = 0.90–1.00. Solid lines represent Al segregation while dashed lines represent N segregation, with black for 1# sample, blue for 2#, and red for 3#. All segregation ratios increase with rising Z_s, and the increase becomes sharper at the late solidification stage (high Z_s). The segregation degree of N is obviously higher than that of Al for all three samples. This figure supports the conclusion that higher Al content promotes AlN inclusion precipitation, and the significantly reduced N solubility in the 3# sample delays the solidification fraction for AlN inclusion precipitation.

This line chart presents the relationship between solidification fraction $z_s$ (x-axis, ranging 0.80 to 1.00) and logarithmic values of TiN precipitation equilibrium constant $\log K_{\text{TiN}}$ (solid lines) and actual activity product $\log Q_{\text{TiN}}$ (dashed lines, y-axis, ranging -4.5 to -2.5) for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples: 1# with ~1 wt% Al (black lines), 2# with ~3 wt% Al (blue lines), 3# with ~5 wt% Al (red lines). The intersection point of each sample's $\log K_{\text{TiN}}$ and $\log Q_{\text{TiN}}$ curves marks the solidification fraction at which TiN inclusions initiate precipitation, measured as 0.89 for 1#, 0.93 for 2#, and 0.94 for 3#. The observed trend demonstrates that increasing Al content delays TiN precipitation, as precipitation occurs at a higher, later-stage solidification fraction with rising Al addition. This figure supports the core conclusion that elevated Al content modifies the solidification route of the tested low-density steels, leading to delayed TiN inclusion precipitation.

This chart illustrates the relationship between logK<sub>MnS</sub>, logQ<sub>MnS</sub>, and solidification fraction z<sub>s</sub> for three Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel samples: 1# with 0.87 wt% Al, 2# with 2.77 wt% Al, 3# with 4.70 wt% Al. For each sample, the solid line stands for logK<sub>MnS</sub> and the dashed line stands for logQ<sub>MnS</sub>, their intersection point indicates the critical solidification fraction at which MnS inclusions begin to precipitate. The intersection points locate at z<sub>s</sub>=0.77 for 1# sample, 0.81 for 2# sample, and 0.85 for 3# sample. The core conclusion supported by this figure is that higher Al content increases the critical solidification fraction for MnS precipitation, meaning increasing Al content delays the precipitation of MnS inclusions during steel solidification, which is consistent with the solidification route calculation results of the tested steels.

This chart presents the calculated lattice mismatch values between different composite inclusion pairs in Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steel. The x-axis lists four inclusion pairs: Al₂O₃/MnS, AlN/MnS, TiN/MnS, and Al₂O₃/AlN, while the y-axis represents mismatch degree in percentage. Three effectiveness zones for heterogeneous nucleation are divided: most effective zone (mismatch <6%), moderate effective zone (6% ≤ mismatch ≤12%), and invalid zone (mismatch >12%). The measured mismatch values for the four pairs are 8.41%, 8.94%, 11.65%, and 11.66% respectively, all falling into the moderate effective zone. These results confirm that Al₂O₃, AlN, and TiN can act as heterogeneous nucleation sites for MnS, and Al₂O₃ can also provide nucleation sites for AlN, explaining the formation of observed composite inclusions in the studied steel.

This schematic illustrates the evolution of inclusions during smelting and solidification of Fe-15Mn-xAl-C low-density steels with three different Al contents (1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%). It shows the formation process of inclusions: primary single-phase inclusions including Al₂O₃, AlN, MnS, TiN precipitate first, then later-precipitated phases nucleate on earlier formed inclusions via heterogeneous nucleation to form composite inclusions such as Al₂O₃-MnS, AlN-MnS, TiN-MnS, and Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS. As Al content increases, the amount of single Al₂O₃ inclusions decreases while AlN inclusions increase, and the types of composite inclusions change accordingly: no Al₂O₃-AlN-MnS composite inclusions exist in the 5 wt% Al sample due to the low content of single Al₂O₃. This figure supports the conclusion that Al content modifies the solidification route, thus regulating the type, morphology, and composition of inclusions in the steels.
